request: javac is not recognized as a command in my terminal. Just installed Java this morning.

replies.message: Sounds like PATH isn't set. What OS are you on?
Windows 11
Got it — added JAVA_HOME and PATH entries. Run `javac --version` to verify.
Works! Thanks!

request: My nested for loop prints the multiplication table but the columns aren't lined up. Some entries are 1 digit and some are 3.

replies.message: Use `printf("%4d", n)` instead of `print(n)` — it pads to 4 spaces.
That worked, thank you! Why doesn't println accept format strings?
println is a shortcut. The full machinery lives in printf / format.

request: I missed last week's lab for medical reasons. Can someone walk me through what we covered?
ConcurrentModificationException keeps showing up when I try to remove odd numbers from an ArrayList in a for-each loop.

replies.message: Classic. Java's enhanced for can't modify the collection while iterating. Switch to an Iterator and call `it.remove()`, or use `list.removeIf(...)`.
Tried removeIf — works perfectly. Way cleaner than my old code.
Glad! removeIf was added in Java 8 — a lot of older tutorials don't show it.

request: Why does ArrayList<int> not compile but ArrayList<Integer> does?

replies.message: Generics don't work with primitives — they need objects. Integer is the wrapper class for int.
Got it, autoboxing handles the conversion?
Exactly.

request: My autograder shows 'Compilation Failed' but I can compile locally just fine.

replies.message: Did you push everything? Especially any new files you might have added?

request: Trying to write a recursive method that reverses a string but I keep getting StackOverflowError no matter how short the input is.

replies.message: Sounds like the base case never fires. Can you share your method signature and base case?
public String reverse(String s) { return reverse(s) + s.charAt(0); }
Aha — your recursive call is `reverse(s)`, not `reverse(s.substring(1))`. So you never get closer to the base case.
Oh my god. Thank you.

request: I think I have the right idea for the Towers of Hanoi recursion but my output has duplicate moves.

replies.message: Common bug: are you printing inside both the recursive call and the helper? Likely calling the helper twice.
Found it — I had two `move()` calls between the recursive steps when I only needed one.

request: Can I get an extension on HW4? Family emergency this weekend.
Trying to override toString in Triangle but the parent Shape.toString is what gets called when I print.

replies.message: Did you put @Override on it? If so, what's the exact signature?
public String toString(int sides) — I added an int param since triangles have 3 sides.
There's your bug — that's overloading, not overriding. The signature has to match the parent's exactly (no params).

request: Can a constructor call super() AND this()?

replies.message: Both have to be the first statement, so no — you can only call one. What are you trying to do?

request: How do I push my code? I tried git push but it asks for a username and password.

replies.message: GitHub deprecated password auth. Set up an SSH key or use a personal access token. There's a guide on the course website.
SSH worked, thanks!

request: I'm really struggling overall and don't know if I should drop the course. Can we talk?

replies.message: Of course. Let's set up a 1-on-1. Are you free Thursday at 3pm?
Note for staff: flagging this for follow-up after our meeting.

request: What's the difference between == and .equals() for Strings? I read about it but it's still confusing.

replies.message: == checks if two references point to the same object. .equals() checks content. For Strings you basically always want .equals().
Why does `"foo" == "foo"` return true then?
String literals are interned in Java — both "foo"s point to the same pooled object. That's a special case, don't rely on it.
